question: How does the electrical conductivity of ZrB2-5 vol% graphene composites compare to graphene-free ZrB2 when tested at room temperature?
answer: The sources do not provide specific information regarding the electrical conductivity of ZrB2-5 vol% graphene composites compared to graphene-free ZrB2 at room temperature. However, it is noted that composites with graphene showed higher electrical conductivity than the reference ZrB2 sample, with a clear anisotropy observed: electrical conductivity was significantly higher in the direction parallel to the graphene alignment [1][3]. The exact value for a 5 vol% graphene composite is not specified in the provided sources.

chunks.text:
4. Summary and conclusions
This  work investigates the  fabrication  of  ZrB2-SiC/graphene composites  without  sintering  aids  using  Spark  Plasma  Sintering  (SPS), focusing  on  densification,  mechanical  properties,  and  the  resulting anisotropy from graphene alignment.
Despite sintering curves suggesting full consolidation, relative density  measurements  and  SEM  observations  revealed  the  presence  of porosity,  indicating  that  sintering  activators  might  be  necessary  for ZrB2. Composites with SiC had agglomerates, while those with graphene showed partial decomposition of the graphene due to the high sintering temperature (2000 ◦ C).
Regarding  mechanical  properties,  all  composites  demonstrated higher hardness than the pure ZrB2  reference. The highest values for hardness  (1750  HV10)  and  fracture  toughness  (3.5  MPa  m 1 / 2 )  were achieved for the composite with 40 vol% of SiC. In graphene composites, hardness measured 1400 HV10 perpendicular and 1600 HV10 parallel to  alignment,  while  fracture  toughness  remained  low  (2.1 -2.3  MPa m 1 / 2 ).
All  composites  exhibited  superior  electrical  conductivity  than  the ZrB2 reference.  In  graphene  composites,  a  clear  anisotropy  was observed: electrical conductivity was significantly higher in the direction parallel to the graphene alignment (3.37*10 GLYPH<0> 4 to 9.12*10 GLYPH<0> 4 μ S/m), exceeding the reference sample by several orders of magnitude.
T. Cygan et al.
Fig. 10. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern for composites with a) 30 % SiC, b) 2 % Gn.
To conclude, it can be stated that.

3.1 Diffuse interface model with or without graphene
To identify the underlying mechanisms of temperature dependence and interfacial thermal conductance of graphene/ZrB2 composites, we inspected diffuse interface models with or without graphene at different temperatures in Figure 1. Previous theoretical calculations had illustrated that the Zr-terminated (0001) and (1010) surfaces were thermodynamically more favorable than other Zrterminated counterparts in ZrB 2 . 30 At high temperatures (below the melting point of the buck ZrB 2 ), disordered grain boundaries enhance phonon scattering and then generate an increased thermal resistance (Figure 1A). Graphene layers prepared by the exfoliated method are almost free of defects and grain boundaries. Extensive literature reported that exfoliated graphene layers with atomically thin tend to adhere to the ceramic grain boundary via vdWs force. 19 Given the fact that phonons are the main heat carriers of graphene and ceramic materials, the electronic thermal conductivity can be negligible in graphene and ZrB2 materials. 31 The rough edges of 2D materials, such as graphene, and its interlayers are typi- cally much less sensitive to phonon scattering than temperature fluctuation. 32 The graphene heterostructure with or without defects has almost the same temperature jump ∆ T at the heterogeneous interface. 17 That is, graphene layers with or without intrinsic defects (or crystal boundaries or rough edges) expand nearly the same amount with the same temperature fluctuation. So we dislodge phonon scattering from defect scattering and rough edges of graphene layers. The vdWs force between graphene and ZrB2 ceramic particles produces perturbation of the atomic bonding in the graphene near the ZrB2 /graphene/ZrB2 heterogeneous interface. This short-range vdWs interaction only affects the graphene layers (1 # , 3 # ) adjacent to the ZrB2 grain boundaries, which is consistent with the reported literature.

3. Results and discussion
Results of electrical conductivity for produced composites are presented in Fig. 9. Due to the low value (6.38*10 GLYPH<0> 7 μ S/m) and readability of the figure (Fig. 9), the bar for the reference sample is not shown. For all  composites,  measured  values  are  higher  than  for  pure  zirconium diboride specimen, but vary one to another depending on the series. Sinters  with  SiC  presented  just  a  slight  increase,  to  9.86*10 GLYPH<0> 6 -3.19

Fig. 6. Influence of SiC/Gn addition on Young ' s modulus of produced ZrB2 composites.
*10 GLYPH<0> 5 μ S/m. This is consistent with the rule of mixture as silicon carbide is characterised by lower electrical resistivity than zirconium diboride. In  the  case  of  graphene,  for  both  series  the  measured  electrical  conductivity  was  higher  than  for  SiC  composites,  and  clear  differences depending on the direction were also observed. Examination performed perpendicular to alignment revealed values in the range of 6.44*10 GLYPH<0> 5 to 1.22*10 GLYPH<0> 4 μ S/m (for 2 vol% addition). On the other hand, the highest electrical conductivity was measured for the series with Gn parallel to alignment, with values in the range of 3.37*10 GLYPH<0> 4 to 9.12*10 GLYPH<0> 4 μ S/m (once again for 2 vol% addition). 3. Results and discussion
Fig. 8. Influence of SiC/Gn addition on fracture toughness of produced ZrB2 composites.
Fig.  9. Influence  of  SiC/Gn  addition  on  electrical  conductivity  of  produced ZrB2  composites.
it is a few order of magnitude increase when compared to the reference specimen. The results show a clear anisotropy of electrical conductivity, which  is  a  direct  consequence  of  the  alignment  of  graphene  flakes perpendicular to the pressing direction in the SPS furnace. During the SPS process, the uniaxial pressing causes the graphene flakes, which have  a  high  aspect  ratio,  to  orient  themselves  perpendicular  to  the pressing direction. This creates a texture, that facilitates the easy flow of electrons parallel to the flakes, resulting in higher electrical conductivity. At the same time, in the direction perpendicular to the flakes, current flow is hindered due to the higher resistance of the ceramic matrix, leading to a distinctly lower conductivity. This microstructural alignment is therefore the key mechanism responsible for the anisotropy of electrical properties in the tested composites. This shows that graphene, with its exceptional electrical resistivity, is an ideal material for use in composites where it is important to improve electrical properties and maintain high mechanical properties.
Fig. 10 presents XRD patterns for composites with 30 vol% SiC and 2 vol% Gn. A thoroughly analysis of the phase composition, showed only the presence of zirconium diboride and silicon carbide. No new phases were detected during the performed examination. It should be noted, however, that for the adopted X-ray diffraction device, the detection threshold is roughly 2 %.

KEYWORDS
bonding configurations, ceramic composites, heat transfer, ordered graphene, thermal converger
dynamic flow of the high-speed aircraft surface, generates a vast chasm between rapid energy-dissipation requirement and ultrahigh thermal conductance. Graphene has an ultrahigh in-plane thermal conductivity of more than 5 × 10 3 W ∙ m -1 ∙ K -1 , which is higher than that of metal and graphite. 2 The concurrent superiority of scalability, large surface-to-volume ratio, and high-temperature stability enables graphene to be the best heat reinforcement of UHTCs. 3 The excellent features of dispersion and assembly further make it easy to be incorporated into the UHTCs matrix. These exotic properties ignite strong interest in accelerating the heat transfer of ZrB 2 composites by constructing external heat-dissipation channels with graphene at ZrB2 grain boundaries. Yet graphene layers were either exfoliated from imperfect graphite powder or grown by chemical vapor deposition. 4 The in-plane thermal conductivity of embedded graphene layers, owing to the phonon Umklapp scattering of a heterogenous interface, decreasing from ∼ 10 2 W ⋅ m -1 ⋅ K -1 at room temperature to ∼ 10 1 W ⋅ m -1 ⋅ K -1 at high temperature. 5 Many worksofliterature have reported that the universal phonon scattering of heterogenous interface and Kapitza thermal resistance of graphene layers cause a reversed correlation between thermal conductance and temperature. 6,7 Interestingly, few researchers have found that the thermal conductivity of ZrB 2 composites with or without graphene reduces the diverse amount with the same temperature fluctuation. 5,8 Therefore, the underlying mechanisms behind the temperature-dependent heat transfer deterioration as the highly anisotropic phonon transport of embedded graphene layers remain elusive.

2. Thermal properties of graphene/ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites
Graphene is a promising conductive material with a carrier mobility of up to 1.5 × 10 4 cm 2 /(V s) and thermal conductivity of up to 5.3 × 10 3 W/(m K) at room temperature, making it an ideal thermoelectric material. Graphene is used to enhance the thermal conductivity of the matrix, accelerate heat loss in the local superheated area, and improve its service life.
Simonenko et al. incorporated 2% graphene into HfB2-30% (volume ratio) SiC, and its thermal conductivity increased from 113 to 164 W/(m K), which is an increase of 45%. 53 Cheng et al. found that in a ZrB2-SiC/graphene composite system, the thermal diffusion coefficient, thermal conductivity, and specific heat coefficient significantly improved after the addition of graphene. 54 Excessive addition not only causes the thermal conductivity of the material to decrease, mainly because the graphene itself will aggregate, resulting in a decrease in thermal conductivity, but also causes the grain size of the ceramic matrix to decrease and the porosity to increase.
Graphene is an excellent thermal conductivity interface that can form a good thermal conductivity interface in the ceramic matrix and build an efficient 3D heat transfer channel, thereby improving thermal conductivity. In addition, the introduction of graphene provides a more efficient method for phonon transport, thus improving thermal conductivity.

A B S T R A C T
As the total thermal conduction in diborides comprises both electronic and phononic component,   electrical conductivity ( σ e) at room temperature  has  also  been  assessed.  The  electrical  conductivity  and Seebeck coefficient (S) of the HfB2-ZrB2-based composites was measured in the He atmosphere at room temperature using ZEM-3M10 apparatus (ULVAC-RICO Inc.). The figure of  merit  ( ZT = S 2 σ eT κ )  was  calculated, Table  2,  from  thermoelectric  parameters  (electrical  conductivity  and Seebeck  coefficient).  Seebeck  coefficient  is  observed  to  be  negative, suggesting n-type semiconductor behavior, with electrons to be dominant charge carriers in all the composites, and following similar trend of electrical conductivity at room temperature with maximum of -3.57 V/K observed for HZ20S6C. The power factor is ~6 × 10 -5 Wm -1 K 2 at room temperature for Z20S, HZ20S and HZ20S6C composites (Table 2) except for H20S (where the presence of HfB2  phase dominates). On the other hand, ZT value for Z20S is ~43 times to that of H20S. After incorporation of CNT, all thermoelectric parameters in HZ20S6C are observed to increase ~4 -6% as compared to that of HZ20S. Low ZT value in ~1 × 10 -4 Wm -1 K -1 range (due to high κ ) for these composites is not sufficient for thermoelectric applications at room temperature.

3.2. Thermal and electrical properties

B 4 C, Thermal conductivity (W m GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 ) = 20 [30]. SiC, Electrical conductivity (S/m) = 70-140 [48]. SiC, Thermal conductivity (W m GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 ) = 100 [41]. LaB 6, Electrical conductivity (S/m) = 1x10 7 [49]. LaB 6, Thermal conductivity (W m GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 ) = 110 [42]
Fig. 8. Electrical conductivity vs. Temperature in the two FGM samples, corresponding equivalent non-graded composites, and the composites having composition corresponding to each layer of the FGM.

3.2 The bonding configurations of ZrB2/graphene/ZrB2 heterostructure
Research results have shown that interfacial thermal conductance is strongly influenced by interface bonding, and the existence of a saturated bonding rate can make the interface thermal conductance reach the predicted value in the acoustic mismatch model. 47 Therefore, the interface thermal conductance can be controlled by adjusting the Zr-C interfacial bonding rate below the saturated interfacial bonding ratio. Only six interfacial configurations with different concentrations of Zr-C interfacial bonding (1-6 vol.%) are constructed for each interface. A critical consideration is the graphene layers' thermal expansion coefficient (TEC), should match that of the ZrB 2 ceramic that it contacts. The in-plane thermal expansion coefficient of graphene (ranges from negative values to 1.2 × 10 -5 K -1 ) is ∼ 2 times that of ZrB 2 (5.9 × 10 -6 K -1 ) at room temperature. 48,49 Andthermal expansion coefficient depends on the interfacial interactions with heterogeneous materials. Interlayer phonon scattering of graphene reduces the in-plane thermal conductivity to approach the value of graphite when the thickness is increased to only about four layers. 50 So three-layer graphene was embedded into the ZrB 2 matrix to investigate the correlation between interlayer phonon scattering and interfacial thermal resistance. Owing to the outer 1 # and 3 # layers being covalent bonding while the inner 2 # layer is held by vdWs force, the expansion of each graphene layer is different. The interfacial thermal resistance of ZrB 2 /graphene plays a leading role in hindering thermal transport at low volume concentrations of graphene, while graphene-graphene (1 # /2 # or 2 # /3 # ) contact resistance becomes dominant in hindering the thermal transport at high content.

A B S T R A C T
The  experimental  electrical  conductivity  at  room  temperature  is maximum for Z20S (~5.69 × 10 6 Sm -1 ), which is ~ 2.85 times (~1.99 × 10 6 Sm -1 ) to that of H20S owing to inherently high electrical conductivity of  pure ZrB2 (~1.0 × 10 7 Sm -1 [8,9]) than HfB2 (~9.1 × 10 6 Sm -1 [9]). However, SiC is the least electrically conducting phase (~10 2 Sm -1 [9]) in all the four compositions, thus, acting as electronic scattering centers. The σ e is comparable (~5.6 -5.7 × 10 6 Sm -1 ) for Z20S, HZ20S and HZ20S6C (~0.01 × 10 6 Sm -1 ). To obtain complex electron interaction in HZ20S and HZ20S6C, the effects of porosity and grain size are normalized where on the other hand the formation of solid solution is taken into consideration which restricts the electron motion due to the hindrance by intermixed electrons of Hf/ZrB2  [31] . Thus, a slight increase in electrical conductivity (~5%) of HZ20S is a combined effect of solid solution formation and presence of porosity ( < 4%). The electrical conductivity further increases ~4% (~5.72 × 10 6 Sm -1 ) on addition of CNT ( σ e~10 4 Sm -1 ) to HZ20S.

Fig. 22 [165,166].
A few studies have been carried out to estimate the thermal conductivity of ZrB2 at room temperature and at elevated temperatures (till 2000 ◦ C), yet no clear trend of increasing or decreasing thermal conductivity with temperature is observed. Samsonov et al. [167] reported thermal conductivity of 60 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 for ZrB2 at 1000 ◦ C, which steadily increased to 130 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 at 2000 ◦ C while as Branscomb and Hunter estimated a value of 105 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 at 200 ◦ C which decreased to 91 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 at 1000 ◦ C [168]. This discrepancy in thermal conductivity is probably due to the dependence of thermal diffusivity, which is the most important parameter in calculating thermal conductivity indirectly, on factors like composition, grain size, contact resistance between grains, porosity and purity etc. [61] Thus, a consensus on the intrinsic thermal conductivity value of ZrB2 is actually missing.

99.5 ZrB2/SiCp, 99 SiCw/Cg
Thompson et al . [20] and Andrievskii et al . [11] also investigated ZrB 2 with carbon additions. The k values increased with increasing temperature, indicating that the diboride was  the  primary  influence  on k .  For  Andrievskii et  al .,  26  vol%  carbon  was  added  as graphite, which reduced the conductivity approximately 10  W/m-K at all temperatures [11]. Thompson et al . added carbon in the form of phenolic resin, resulting in 1 and 3  wt% amorphous carbon after pyrolysis. After hot pressing, graphitic carbon was identified at the grain boundaries. ZrB 2 containing 1  wt% C had k values lower than pure ZrB 2 . They hypothesized that graphite was aligned, with its basal plane parallel to the grain boundaries, thus disrupting phonon transport between ZrB 2 grains. However, k did not continue to decrease when C content was increased to 3  wt% C due to the segregation of W into ZrC after testing up to 2000°C. The ZrC phase, produced due to carbon additions, served as a sink for W, reducing the W content of the ZrB 2 grains and boosting the overall k of the composite [20].
The addition of ZrC to ZrB 2 typically reduces k . Andrievskii et al . evaluated both 5 and 50 vol% ZrC from 100 to 900°C, while Fridlender et al . added 20, 40, and 45 vol% ZrC and tested up to 2150 or 2200°C. The higher ZrC additions resulted in k values between 40 and 55  W/m-K, while the lower additions resulted in k values between 64 and 80  W/m-K, despite the different testing temperatures and compositions. The differences, and the decrease in k with increasing ZrC content, were probably due to the variability in k values for ZrC (20-45  W/m-K at room temperature and increasing to 30-50  W/m-K at 2000°C), which are well below the values for pure ZrB 2 [70].

3.1 Diffuse interface model with or without graphene
In the heat flow direction fromZrB2 to graphene, ZrB 2 /graphene heterostructure has an interfacial thermal conductance of 40.3 MW ∙ m -2 ∙ K -1 (pink dots), which is higher than that of graphene/ZrB 2 heterostructure 36.6 MW ∙ m -2 ∙ K -1 in the opposite direction (blue dots). The thermal rectification factors can be expressed by 37

(B) The thermal rectification factor η of ZrB2 /graphene/ZrB2 heterostructure at different temperatures. <!-- formula-not-decoded -->
The underlying mechanisms for thermal rectification are shown in Figure 2D. Case 1 is that heat flow from ZrB2 to graphene, 1# while Case 2 is that the reversed heat flow direction from graphene3 # to ZrB2 . In the λ curves, the higher line is the thermal conductance of ZrB 2 , while the lower line is the cross-section thermal conductance of graphene. Their interface-dominated mechanisms of phonon transmission, caused by the enhanced anharmonicity at the ZrB2 /graphene 1# and graphene3 # /ZrB 2 heterogeneous interfaces, will be analyzed in detail later.

9.1. Thermal expansion, thermal conductivity and electrical conductivity
In a different work, the electrical conductivity of ZrB2 composite decreased with increasing SiC, since SiC exhibits higher electrical resistivity compared to ZrB2 [287]. Furthermore, SiC (up to 20 vol%) containing ZrB2 exhibits still better electrical conductivity. On a slightly different perspective, He et al. [371] studied the effect of ZrB2-20SiC content on the electrical and mechanical properties of MoSi2. Their work indicated that the electrical resistivity of MoSi2 decreased with increase in the ZrB2-20SiC content; which again highlights the conductivity aspect of ZrB2-SiC ceramic composites. Overall, it has been realized that incorporation of optimized contents of disilicides and carbides to transition metal di-borides helps improve the conductivity of transition metal borides [137,147,372].

A B S T R A C T
This  study  presents  new  findings  on  ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  composites,  where  silicon  carbide  and graphene were incorporated into a zirconium diboride matrix. The research focused on the densification process, and the mechanical and electrical properties of the produced materials. A novel aspect of this work was the investigation of the influence of graphene alignment on composite properties. The results indicated that despite SPS curves suggested full consolidation, the final relative density was lower than expected, and SEM observations revealed the presence of pores in the microstructure. This suggests that additional sintering activators may be necessary to achieve full densification of ZrB2-based materials. Furthermore, high consolidation temperature (2000 ◦ C)  caused  partial  decomposition  of  graphene,  with  only  some  flakes  preserving  their  structure.  Agglomerates of silicon carbide particles were also observed in SiC composites. Regarding mechanical properties, all composites showed a significant increase in hardness compared to pure ZrB2. The highest hardness (1750 HV10) and fracture toughness (3.5 MPa m 1 / 2 ) were achieved for the composite with 40 vol% SiC. For electrical properties, all composites showed enhanced conductivity than the reference ZrB2 sample. Notably, the graphene composites exhibited a clear anisotropy. Specimens tested parallel to the graphene alignment showed a conductivity increase of a few orders of magnitude. In conclusion, this study demonstrates that SPS is an effective method for consolidating ZrB2-based composites without sintering aids, leading to significant improvements in hardness and electrical conductivity. However, achieving full densification may require the use of activators. The observed anisotropy of electrical properties in graphene composites highlights their potential for applications where directional conductivity is desired. This work opens new paths for better understanding ultra-high temperature ceramic-based composites.

question: What densification results are achieved for ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composites sintered at 1800°C using spark plasma sintering?
answer: The densification result achieved for ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composites sintered at 1800°C using spark plasma sintering is a relative density of 99.96% [9].

chunks.text:
4.4. Spark Plasma Sintering
In the case of spark plasma sintering (SPS) of ZrB2 ceramics, the effect of processing parameters (sintering time and sintering temperature) and powder particle size (nano- or micron- sized) on the densification has been shown in Fig. 7b [120]. It can be noted that the densification rate is extremely high for the smaller particle size (≤25 nm) and near-theoretical densification of ZrB2 is attained even at very low sintering temperatures (below 1700 °C) and shorter sintering times of 16 min or lesser. The spark plasma sintered di-borides are usually observed to possess fine microstructure and good combination of properties. In order to address some issues related to non-uniform densification (and properties) primarily due to fast heating rate and lesser holding times, recently we had explored the application of multi-stage spark plasma sintering (MSS-SPS) schedule, which involves holding at one or more intermediate temperatures for few minutes before reaching the final sintering temperatures [98,173-179]. Keeping the total holding time constant (i.e. lesser holding time at the final sintering temperature), more uniform densification and finer microstructures were achieved for ZrB2-SiC-TiSi2 [175] and TiB2-TiSi2 composites [98], resulting in still improved mechanical properties (as compared to similar compositions sintered via the usual single-step SPS route).

a b s t r a c t
In this paper spark plasma sintering (SPS) of silicon carbide and multi-walled carbon nanotube reinforced zirconium diboride ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites is reported. Systematic investigations on the effect of reinforcement type (SiC and CNTs) and content (10-40 vol.% SiC and 2-6 vol.% CNTs) on densification behavior, microstructure development, and mechanical properties (microhardness, biaxial flexural strength, and indentation fracture toughness) are presented. With the similar SPS processing parameters (1900 ◦ C, 70 MPa pressure, and 15 min soaking time), near-full densification (>99% relative density) was achieved with 10-40% SiC (in ZrB2-SiC) and 4-6% CNT (in ZrB2-CNT) reinforced composites. The SiC and CNT reinforcement further improved the indentation fracture toughness of the composites through a range of toughening mechanisms, including particle shearing, crack deflection at the particlematrix interface, and grain pull-outs for ZrB2-SiC composites, and CNT pull-outs and crack deflection in ZrB2-CNT composites.

1. Introduction
Spark plasma sintering (SPS) route was employed for preparation of quadruplet ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC -Cf ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMC). Zirconium diboride and silicon carbide powders with a constant ZrB2:SiC volume ratio of 4:1 were selected as the baseline. Mixtures of ZrB2 -SiC were co-reinforced with zirconium carbide (ZrC: 0 -10 vol%) and carbon /uniFB01 ber (Cf: 0 -5 vol%), taking into account a constant ratio of 2:1 for ZrC:Cf components. The sintered composite samples, processed at 1800 °C for 5 min and 30 MPa punch press under vacuumed atmosphere, were characterized by densitometry, /uniFB01 eld emission scanning electron microscopy, energy dispersive spectroscopy, X-ray di /uniFB00 ractometry as well as mechanical tests such as hardness and /uniFB02 exural strength measurements. The results veri /uniFB01 ed that the composite co-reinforced with 5 vol% ZrC and 2.5 vol% Cf had the optimal characteristics, i.e., it reached a relative density of 99.6%, a hardness of 18 GPa and a /uniFB02 exural strength of 565 MPa.
less-volatile borosilicate glasses, but will also promote the mechanical properties [23,24].
It is established that adding 20 vol% SiC to ZrB2 will result in achievement of optimal high-temperature thermo-mechanical application for ZrB2 -SiC composites [25]. Overally, excellent densi /uniFB01 cation behavior, oxidation and thermal shock resistance, because of the smaller coe /uniFB03 cient of thermal expansion compared to ZrB2 and ZrC, make silicon carbide one of the most important materials for making e /uniFB03 cient composites with ZrB2 [26,27].

4. Conclusions
Spark plasma sintering processing was used to produce dense short /uniFB01 bre-reinforced ceramic composites based on ZrB2 with a carbon /uniFB01 bres content of 45 vol%. The relative density of 96.7%, achieved for samples sintered at 1900 °C, is signi /uniFB01 cantly higher than analogous specimens produced by conventional hot pressing, which achieved only 85% of the theoretical density. Careful control of the sintering parameters, such as the heating rate, maximum temperature and holding time resulted in materials with near full density and no appreciable damage of the carbon /uniFB01 bres.
- It has also been assessed that above a critical temperature (> 1900 °C), set by the eutectic melt met in the ZrB2-SiC-C system, extreme /uniFB01 bre degradation occurs, with associated brittle behaviour of the composite. It has also been observed that slight deviations in the density level have a strong impact on the fracture behaviour.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx. doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2017.05.029.

4. Conclusions
Spark plasma sintering was successfully used to sinter fine-grained ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites to near-full densification (>99% relative density). Detailed analysis of punch displacement during SPS sintering indicated that the densification is favored for the ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composite samples in early stage I (particle re-distribution stage) and final stage III (densification starting at earlier times and at lower temperatures). While the fine grains (<2 𝛍 m) were retained in ZrB2-SiC composites, some grain growth with grain size in the range 5-10 𝛍 m was observed for ZrB2-CNT composites. Raman spectroscopy analysis and high magnification SEM micrographs indicated that the multi-walled CNTs were retained in ZrB2-CNT composite samples sintered at high sintering temperature of 1900 ◦ C. The SiC and CNT reinforcement further improved the indentation fracture toughness of the composites through a range of toughening mechanisms, including particle shearing, crack deflection at the particle-matrix interface, and grain pull-outs for ZrB2-SiC composites, and CNT pull-outs and crack deflection in ZrB2-CNT composites.

1. Introduction
Although finding an appropriate composition is one of the most critical challenges for achieving the best relative density, hardness, and flexural strength, selecting a proper sintering process and its parameters is another essential issue. ZrB2-20 vol% SiC has been studied as a base composition with a ZrB2: SiC volume ratio of 4:1 and a ZrC: Cf volume ratio of 2:1. Optimum densification and mechanical characteristics, 99.6% relative density, 18.1 GPa hardness and 564.5 MPa flexural strength, have been reported for ZrB2-18.5 vol% SiC-5.0 vol% ZrC-2.5 vol% Cf composite [26]. Among several sintering processes such as spark plasma sintering (SPS) [27-33], pressureless sintering (PS) [34], hot pressing (HP) [35-37], and reactive hot pressing (RHP) [38,39], samples produced by the SPS process were found to have relatively higher mechanical characteristics [40-45]. Because of strong bonding in carbides and borides, it is difficult to achieve superb sintering characteristics by conventional sintering processes [46-50]. composites was assessed by weight change, oxide layer thickness measurements, surface and cross-sectional microstructure observation.

4.2.2. Mechanical behavior
Incorporation  of  5  wt  %  C3N4 in  ZrB2-25  vol  %  SiC  composite resulted in an enhanced densification (from 95% to fully dense) during spark plasma sintering [145]. The removal of oxide impurities by C3N4 not only improved densification, but also refined the microstructure as well, yielding better mechanical properties while forming in situ BN and ZrC [145]. Vickers hardness increased from ~17 GPa to ~21 GPa and flexural strength also enhanced from ~460 MPa to ~580 MPa. Apart from achieving an enhanced densification, in situ formation of BN and ZrC yielded higher fracture toughness due to crack deflection and crack bridging  mechanisms  (6.1  MPam 1/2 as  compared  to  that  of  4.5 MPam 1/2 )  [145].  10  vol  %  CNT  and  20  vol  %  SiC  reinforcement increased the flexural strength of ZrB2  (i.e., from 322.8 MPa) to 347.6 MPa and 378.7 MPa, respectively. However, with synergistic A. Nisar et al.

A B S T R A C T
ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composite was fully densified with the addition of tantalum (from 2.5 to 10 wt%) using multi stage spark plasma sintering (MSSPS). The microstructural phases of core-ZrB2  and rim-(Zr,Ta)B2  along with ZrO2,  (Zr,Ta)C  phases  were  observed  with  the  addition  of  tantalum  refractory  metal  to  ZrB2 -SiC.  Both  the nanoindentation and Vickers hardness of ZrB2  composites exhibited similar trend and almost same hardness values.  However,  maximum  nanoindentation  hardness  (18.62  GPa)  and  elastic  modulus  (451.60  GPa)  were measured for ZrB2-20 vol% SiC with 10 wt% Ta. The increase in amount of tantalum reduces the specific weight gain and oxide layer thickness, hence ZrB2-20 vol% SiC with 10 wt% Ta exhibited better oxidation resistance compared to other compositions after oxidation at 1600 ◦ C for 10 h. In all the compositions, the cross-sectional microstructure of oxidation tested samples consists of three different layers such as top passive silica layer, intermediate SiC-depleted layer and unreacted bulk.

ULTRAHIGH-TEMPERATURE CERAMIC BASED ON ZrB2-SiC: PREPARATION AND MAIN PROPERTIES
Previously in cooperation with GNTs FGUP Keldysh Center spark plasma sintering (SPS) has been used to prepare dense ceramic (relative density 92 - 93%) of compositions ZrB2 - 20 wt.% SiC and ZrB 2 - 5 wt.% Si 3 N4 [7] in the form of tablets up to 20 mm in diameter. Indentation into these tablets was used to measure microhardness ( HV = 10 - 18 GPa) and critical stress intensity factor ( K I c = 3.5 - 5.9 MPa·m 1/2 ) with a load of 2 kg. The small size of tablets and the limited working volume of the spark plasma sintering, does not make it possible to measure other ceramic structural properties. Therefore in the next step subsequent experiments were conducted in a hot-pressing (HP) unit with a larger working volume.
Currently the research object is ceramic of the composition ZrB2 - 30 vol.% SiC with different additions (Si 3 N4, TiSi 2 , ZrSi 2 , MoSi2), and experimental data have been obtained for the main thermophysical and strength properties of this ceramic over a wide temperature range.

question: What is the oxidation resistance and preferential oxidation mechanism of multi-component UHTC systems incorporating tungsten carbide (WC) components when exposed to plasma jet at temperatures above 2800°C?
answer:
The provided sources do not contain evidence regarding the oxidation resistance and preferential oxidation mechanism of multi-component UHTC systems incorporating tungsten carbide (WC) components when exposed to plasma jet at temperatures above 2800°C. However, based on the available information:

- The oxidation resistance of multi-component UHTCs (MC-UHTCs) is significantly improved compared to binary UHTCs due to the formation of a complex mixed oxide layer, which suppresses further oxidation [2]. This is attributed to preferential oxidation of certain elements, such as Hf in ternary and Ti in quaternary systems, forming protective mixed complex oxide layers [2].
- The formation of a thermally stable complex protective oxide layer, such as Hf6Ta2O17, has been shown to enhance oxidation resistance in MC-UHTCs [14]. This mechanism involves the preferential oxidation of components at service temperatures, leading to the formation of a protective oxide layer that improves the material's oxidation resistance [14].
- However, specific information about WC components in MC-UHTCs under plasma jet exposure above 2800°C is not covered in the provided sources.

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
Multi-component ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTCs) has garnered attention due to their improved thermo-mechanical and oxidation properties. MC-UHTC was progressively formed by adding a UHTC component at  each  step,  producing  binary,  ternary  and  quaternary  UHTC  systems,  processed  by  spark  plasma  sintering (SPS). We investigate the role of multi-elements and thermochemical stability of the surface oxide layer formed during plasma arc jet exposure ( T > 2500 ◦ C) of MC-UHTCs using core-level X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) correlated with the structural investigation. The results indicate that adding a UHTC component improves oxidation resistance due to the assemblage of refractory phases forming complex mixed oxides via preferential oxidation. XPS spectra of Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, and Hf4f showed that the nature of the chemical bonds switched from metal carbides to metal oxides (and metal oxy-carbides) in the binary system with retained metal carbides in ternary and quaternary UHTCs. The absence of a metal carbide peak in the high-resolution C1s scan of the binary system also supports this finding. The principal complex mixed oxide phases emanated in the exiguous oxidation of MC-UHTCs are quantified using Rietveld analysis. Overall, this study suggests that MC-UHTC is a promising material for possible application in thermal protection systems (TPS).
sharp leading edges in re-entry vehicles. These materials have gathered quick attention because of their remarkable properties, which include enhanced mechanical properties [9 -12], improved fracture toughness [11,13,14],  tribological  performance  [15 -17],  creep  resistance  [18], and low thermal conductivity [11,19 -21].

4. Summary
The MC-UHTCs showed remarkable improvement in the oxidation resistance during plasma exposure testing compared to binary UHTCs. This has been attributed to the preferential oxidation and a reduction in low melting temperature unary oxide content in the surface oxide scale from > 82% in binary UHTC to < 13% in MC-UHTC. A thermally stable complex protective oxide layer of Hf6Ta2O17 (a primary phase) in MCUHTCs has further suppressed the oxide formation. As a result of the relative thermal stability of transition metals and components in the MCUHTCs, preferential oxidation occurs in MC-UHTCs, i.e., Hf in ternary and Ti in quaternary UHTC system, while both are forming the protective mixed complex oxide layer to remarkably improve ( > 40 times) the oxidation resistance over binary and unary UHTC systems. Both XRD and XPS analysis showed retention of metal carbide phase only in MCUHTCs. The study suggests that MC-UHTCs designed to form a mixed complex protective oxide layer of group IV element under service conditions elicit extended potential in extreme space vehicle applications.

5. Conclusion
In this work, a multi-phase multicomponent UHTC, Hf0.61Zr0.26Ti0.13C- (Hf0.30Zr0.32Ti0.38)5Si3, was designed and fabricated. The oxidation mechanism of the multiphase multicomponent UHTC was investigated through experiments in conjunction with thermodynamic analysis. It is found that, during oxidation, the intraphase preferential oxidation of components (i.e., Hf and Zr) and the inter-phase preferential oxidation toward thermodynamically favored phase (i.e., silicide) would occur synchronously. In this process, the intraphase preferential oxidation led to the formation of a nanoscale self-healing oxide skeleton in  carbide  phase.  More  importantly,  the  synergy  of  the  hierarchical preferential oxidation induced an inter-phase migration of preferentially oxidized components (i.e., Hf and Zr) from carbide phase to the preferentially oxidized silicide phase, which facilitated the development of a micrometer-sized  Hf/Zr-rich  oxide  framework.  This  framework  provided additional support for the nano-sized self-healing oxide skeleton, moreover, the as-formed multi-scale hierarchical structured oxide scale offered an effective protection for the material and improved the ablation performance. The ablation test shows that multi-phase multicomponent UHTC with merely 4.4 vol% of silicide demonstrates a superior ablation resistance compared to the single-phase counterpart (a reduction of 64.7% and 62.5% in MAR and LAR, respectively). More broadly, the results obtained in this work could provide a preliminary basis for the design and optimization of the multiphase multicomponent UHTCs.

4.  Potential pathways to address challenges involved with HEUHTCs
Based on the limited available data in the literature [13,30,46,58], a comparison  of  the  mechanical  and  oxidation  resistance  of  mono, binary,  ternary,  and  multi-component  UHTC  carbides  (referred  to  as HE-carbide) is summarized and plotted in Fig. 7. The improvement in the hardness (see Fig. 7) has mainly been attributed to the solid-solutioning in the binary, ternary, and HE-UHTCs. The onset temperature at which the oxide starts forming has remarkably improved from 900 °C in monolith UHTCs to > 1100 °C for HE-UHTCs (Fig. 7 ) . This is possibly due to the formation of the complex, layered structure as well as the composition of the oxide scale in a multi-component system. The innermost oxide layer near the unoxidized sample usually has a dense structure with lesser pores (all filled with molten particles) followed by a porous oxide structure with lesser pores (see Fig. 5). Also, the complex composition of the mixed oxides due to multi-constituents restricts the inward  diffusion  of  O2 providing  oxidation  resistance,  discussed  in section 3.2. However, whether all components in a solid-solution of the HE-UHTC  system  react  with  oxygen  simultaneously  or  preferential oxidation, still needs to be looked upon in both isothermal and highspeed  plasma  jet  conditions.  Also,  this  brings  up  a  question  of  how many  constituents  (four,  five,  six,  or  more)  in  a  multi-component system would be sufficient for enhancement in the properties in the absence of entropy quantification. To get an insight into it, a study on gradually raising the constituents in UHTCs from mono, binary and so on is required along with the delineation of the entropy contributions to the properties.
Fig.  7. Comparison  of  the  hardness  and  onset  of  oxidation  temperature  in various carbides.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), a class of ceramic materials operating above 2000 ◦ C, primarily include borides, carbides, and nitrides of transition metals[1]. As critical materials for extreme environments, their oxidation resistance directly determines their service lifetime  and  reliability  under  high-temperature  oxidative  conditions. HfB ₂ -SiC is a representative UHTC system, widely applied in hypersonic vehicle nose cones, rocket engine nozzles, and other thermal protection components due to its ultra-high melting point ( > 3000 ◦ C) and exceptional oxidation/ablation resistance[2]. In ultra-high-temperature oxidative  environments,  material  surfaces  undergo  intense  oxidation reactions,  forming  porous  or  volatile  oxide  layers  that  lead  to  rapid
* Corresponding author.
** Corresponding author at: College of Aerospace Science and Engineering, National University of Defense Technology, Changsha 410073, China. E-mail address: mr_zla@163.com (L. Zhu).

Applied Surface Science
A companion  publication [7]  revealed  that  MC-UHTCs  have remarkable oxidation resistance compared to unary and binary UHTC attributed to mixed complex oxide phase formation with Hf6Ta2O17 as a major phase. The present work focuses on qualitatively and quantitatively investigating the content and chemical composition of the oxide scale in the binary and MC-UHTCs using core level spectroscopy correlated with phase quantification via Rietveld analysis. Based on the review of various available phase diagrams of a binary and ternary system, the possible combination of oxide phases in the chosen chemistry for the present work is summarized in Table 1.
Fig. 2. XRD pattern of the surface oxide layer formed on the plasma exposed binary and MC-UHTC systems. The colored dots in each XRD pattern represents the experimental raw data, while the lines denote calculated data after Rietveld refinement.
The present study explains how preferential oxidation based on the components added in the UHTC system can improve or deteriorate its oxidation resistance. These findings can enable oxidation-resistant designs for non-oxide multi-component UHTCs.
A. Nisar et al.
Table 2

4. Conclusion
Single-component UHTCs (HfC, ZrC, TiC, TaC, NbC, WC) modified C/C composites were successfully prepared by RMI method. Combined with oxyacetylene ablation test, multi-scale characterization and twoway  coupled  flow-solid  numerical  simulation,  the  ablation  mechanisms of different ceramic systems were systematically revealed. The results show that the denseness of the ceramic layer and the thermal stability of the oxidation products are the key factors determining the ablation performance. Among the single-component UHTCs, HfC (1.42 μ m/s) and ZrC (0.89 μ m/s) exhibit optimal ablation resistance due to the formation  of  dense  oxides  (HfO2/ZrO2)  with  low  volume  expansion (~37 %). While TiC and NbC exhibit increased porosity due to the high mobility of the oxides (5.96 % ~ 7.77 %), WC triggers severe exfoliation due to the low melting point of the oxides and volatile properties (-8.63 μ m/s). However, the high melting point property renders it difficult to form a dense ceramic layer with a single TaSi2.
C/C-HfC-ZrC-TaC-SiC  multi-component  composites  were  designed by elemental interdiffusion and phase synergy. The oxide film thickness increased to 300 μ m after 1000 s of long-time ablation (40 s × 25 times) without obvious crack generation, and the mass weight gain (0.175 mg/ s) and thickness growth (0.701 μ m/s) indicated its dynamic self-healing capability. This work provides an idea for the design of thermal protection  materials  in  extreme  high-temperature  environments, emphasizing  the  screening  of  high-melting-point  UHTCs,  the  synergistic enhancement  of  multi-components  and  the  optimization  of  gradient composite structures. The ablation and thermal shock resistance of the composites can be significantly improved, which lays a theoretical and experimental  foundation  for  the  development  of  long-life  thermal M. Zhang et al.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are a class of ceramic materials that possess a melting point generally greater than 3000 ℃ , have the capability to operate at temperatures around 2000 ℃ (as experienced in hypersonic applications), and are typically composed of carbides, nitrides, and borides of early transition metals [1 -3]. Due to their high-temperature stability and ablation resistance, UHTCs have been widely used in thermal protective systems of aerospace and aviation in the past decades [4,5]. As these developing technologies become more advanced and demanding, UHTCs are under increasing pressure to perform under harsher operating conditions.
Recently, it has been found that multi-component ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTCs) have better oxidation properties than single-component UHTCs [6,7]. MC-UHTC can form a mixed composite oxide protective layer of Group IV elements under oxidation and ablation environment, offering greater potential for extreme space vehicle applications [8 -10]. Zeng et al. [11] proposed a Zr0.8Ti0.2C0.74B0.26 ceramic coating on C/C composites, which realized the ablation resistance of the materials at 3000 ℃ . Kurbatkina et al. [12] prepared (Zr,Ta) B2 UHTC solid solutions, and plasma ablation tests showed the ceramics
* Corresponding author. E-mail address:
ctjyin@hotmail.com (J. Yin).
Received 26 February 2024; Received in revised form 6 July 2024; Accepted 24 July 2024

A B S T R A C T
Designing  ablation-resistant  multi-phase  multicomponent  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  remains challenging due to the absence of the understanding toward their oxidation mechanism. Herein, through the investigations on the ablation behaviors of a multiphase multicomponent UHTC, the mechanism of hierarchical preferential oxidation was elucidated. We found that, during ablation, the intraphase preferential oxidation of elements  and  the  inter-phase  preferential  oxidation  of  thermodynamically  favored  phase  would  occur  synchronously. In this hierarchical preferential oxidation process, the intraphase preferential oxidation led to the formation of nanoscale self-healing oxide skeletons in carbide phase. More importantly, the synergy of the hierarchical preferential oxidation induced an inter-phase migration of preferentially oxidized elements (i.e., Hf/ Zr) to the preferentially oxidized phase (i.e., silicide), which facilitated the development of micrometer-sized Hf/ Zr-rich oxide frameworks. These frameworks and nano-sized skeletons together constituted a multi-scale hierarchical oxide layer, offering an effective protection against scouring of the high-speed hot gas and enhancing the ablation performance.
researches are mainly focus on single-phase multicomponent system, however, multi-phase multicomponent UHTCs are rarely studied so far. Given  the  fact  that  the  multi-phase  approach  has  been  successfully adopted to improve densification [18], mechanical properties [19,20] and oxidation resistance [21,22] of single-phase UHTCs, the multiphase multicomponent  UHTCs  might  achieve  better  and  more  tunable properties.

a b s t r a c t
 T15S15C, Crystallite size (t) of TaC in SPS pellet (nm) Before plasma arc jet test After plasma arc jet test = 46 ± 1. T15S15C, Retention of TaC phase after exposure = ∼ 20%. T15S15C, Thickness of the oxide layer ( 𝛍 m) = 20.1 ± 2.0
subjected to temperature ∼ 3000 ◦ C in presence of oxygen and reported that the formation of oxide layer degrades the stability of TaC both chemically and mechanically. Nieto et al. [10] showed oxidation behaviour of spark plasma sintered TaC-graphene nanoplatelets (1 to 5 vol%) (GNP) composites processed at temperature of 1850 ◦ C under high temperature plasma flow of 30 kW power for 60 s and showed that addition of GNP suppresses formation of oxide layer up to 60%. However, high temperature thermal conductivity for TaC based composites is not available in literature, which is one of the important attributes to survive high temperature exposure.
Improvement of fracture toughness with SiC and CNT addition in TaC-based UHTCs is demonstrated elsewhere [39]. Hence, the scope of the present work is to develop an understanding of the oxidation mechanisms of TaC-SiC-CNT composites to predict the lifetime performance of UHTCs under operational conditions. Oxidation behaviour is analysed in terms of the oxide layer formation during testing. To supplement plasma arc jet testing, thermogravimetric analysis is carried out for 30 min. at 1500 ◦ C in oxygen environment. Thermal conductivity of these materials have been measured using laser flash diffusivity technique and, accordingly, correlation of the heat transfer mechanism during plasma arc jet testing is established. An attempt has been made to associate the synergistic effect of SiC and CNT reinforcement on the performance of TaC-based UHTCs through these investigations.

A B S T R A C T
The desired material properties for ultra-high temperature service require high melting point temperature ( > 3000 ◦ C) [10], high temperature (1700 ◦ C) strength ( > 300 MPa) [10], high thermal conductivity > 14.5 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 [11], and high temperature oxidation resistance, < 80 gm GLYPH<0> 2 over 2  h  at  1400 ◦ C [12].  In  addition,  shock  wave  resistance, thermal shock resistance, and high room-temperature fracture toughness are also highly important to retain structural integrity and good resistance to wear/erosion is also demanded. Therefore, it is imperative to depict the oxidation behavior and formulate the oxidation mechanism involved in UHTCs. Apart from furnace oxidation testing, oxidation tests mimicking  aerothermal  conditions  have  already  been  performed  on these materials, including ZrB2-, HfB2-, TaC-, HfC-, etc. based materials, using oxyacetylene torches [13 -17], plasma arc jets [7,18 -21], subsonic plasma wind tunnels [22], and supersonic plasma wind tunnels [23 -29] simulating the extreme aerothermal conditions.
The collision between orbital debris and spacecraft may cause surface damage in the form of pitting or erosion and can lead to significant damage over time [30]. Moreover, during re-entry of vehicles in the earth ' s atmosphere, kinetically driven secondary particles in the atmosphere cause abrasion/erosion, where temperature can reach > 2200 ◦ C in response to these impacts [6]. Thus, UHTCs require damage tolerance from the launch-vibration fretting, accidental-scratching and high-temperature kinetic impact of particles as well. The formation of tribo-layer often protects the underlying aerospace material under dynamic  sliding  conditions  of  UHTC  ceramics,  making  the  tribological study  an  important  aspect  to  ensure  room  temperature  structural integrity and qualify its erosive damage tolerance.

3.2.  Oxidation resistance in HE-UHTCs
To  date,  only  one  study  which  closely  approximates  the  intense aerothermal conditions have been performed on SPS processed B4(Hf, Mo, Ta, Ti)C with SiC addition [59]. The resistance to oxidation was investigated at moderate temperature (900 °C) for a prolonged time of 50 h and at high temperature (2000 °C) using LPG torch for a short time of 20 s. The weight gain was observed to be negligible; however, after the torch test, HfO2  and TaO2  phases were found on the surface. No change in the weight after being exposed to LPG torch suggests that the time of exposure was possibly not enough for the sample temperature to reach 2000 °C. The microstructural observations or quantification of the oxide phases were not conducted to understand the protective oxidation mechanism [59]. The multi-component constituents in the HE-UHTCs are expected to form a complex oxide scale (as shown in Fig. 5) during the  dynamic  oxidation  testing.  The  complex  oxide  of  Hf6Ta2O17 has shown to improve the oxidation resistance of TaC-HfC solid-solution under the extreme temperature conditions of a plasma jet [72]. The complex oxide does not react with the underlying material making a dense oxide scale. This brings out the need for an in-depth analysis of the  complex  oxides  formed  during  the  oxidation  of  complex  multicomponent HE-UHTCs. In this  regard,  a  focused-ion  beam  (FIB)  can prove to be a powerful tool for the in-depth analysis of the formation of the complex oxide [72]. The sequence of single or complex layer oxide formation, their chemical interaction and presence of sub-surface voids and cracks can be precisely studied through FIB cross-sectioning and correlated with inward oxygen diffusion.

4. Conclusions
Oxidation behaviour of spark plasma sintered TaC-SiC-CNT composites was evaluated through plasma arc jet exposure under heat flux of 2.5 MW/m 2 . Oxidation behaviour is characterised in terms of oxide layer formation, its thickness and residual stresses. After exposure to harsh aerothermal conditions, the structural integrity of these UHTC composites were measured in terms of critical energy release rate ( G IC ) which increases up to 2.5 times ( ∼ 58 to 139 Jm -2 ) with synergistic reinforcement of SiC and CNT in TaC. The protective SiO2 formation with SiC reinforcement in TaC suggested an extension of stability domain which resulted in an improved oxidation resistance. It is elicited that CNT addition improves oxidation resistance of TaC by grain sealing mechanism. Raman spectroscopic analysis elicited the presence of carbonaceous material even after plasma exposure, further supplemented by TEM analysis of T15S15C. In addition, enhanced thermal conductivity with temperature for all composites provides an indication of improved oxidation resistance via heat dissipation. Thermal studies up to 1500 ◦ C have confirmed that the temperature of oxide formation shifts towards higher temperature (from 735 ◦ C to 901 ◦ C) and decrease in the enthalpy of oxide formation (from 4.5 to 3.5 kJ/g) with SiC and CNT reinforcement. Such an observation insinuates synergetic reinforcement of SiC and CNT in enhancing the thermal stability and oxidation resistance of TaC-based UHTC composites.

3.3. Plasma arc jet exposure
Further,  the  cross-sectional  SEM  of  samples  was  conducted  to quantify the thickness of the oxide film formation on the sample surface due to the melting of the Ta2O5  phase, see Fig. 9c and d. The minor cracking in the 50HO-50TO sample vs. wide crack opening in the 70HO30TO sample in the unaffected region elicits that the intense plasma exposure was enough to create thermal stresses, analogous to the strain analysis in Table 1. The thickness of melted and then resolidified oxide film is 210 nm and 297 nm in 50HO-50TO and 70HO-30TO samples. Although there is no significant change in the thickness of melted oxide in both the samples, there are significantly more apparent melted regions in the 70HO-30TO sample. Hence, the 50HO-50TO sample has better  thermal  stability  than  the  70HO-30TO  sample.  In  terms  of improving the oxidation resistance of solid-solution binary UHTCs or MC-UHTCs, it can be anticipated that a composition with equal or higher content of TaC will have a higher probability of Hf6Ta2O17  phase formation during plasma exposure. This complex mixed oxide phase act as a protective layer that suppresses further oxidation. Also, the protective layer in UHTC components is formed due to the preferential oxidation of various  components  at  service  temperature  [16].  The  present  study shows that the complex mixed oxide formation during service ( > 2500 ◦ C) of solid-solution binary UHTCs and MC-UHTCs improves its oxidation resistance due to the thermal stability and mechanical integrity of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase. UHTC composition, thus designed to form a protective and thermally stable Hf6Ta2O17 phase under extreme conditions, will provide new directions to the materials for reusable hypersonic applications.

A B S T R A C T
The advancing aerospace materials requires ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) with robust oxidation and ablation resistance. This study aims to overcome the limitations of conventional carbide UHTCs by developing (Zr1/3Hf1/3Ti1/3)C-SiC  ceramics  by  hot  pressing.  Materials  were  characterized  through  non-isothermal  and isothermal  oxidation  tests,  plasma  ablation  and  multiscale  characterizations.  The  ceramics  achieved  ~98  % theoretical  density,  exhibiting  a  homogeneous  (Zr,Hf,Ti)C  matrix  and  dispersed  SiC.  Key  findings  reveal  an oxidation onset temperature of ~750 ◦ C, with phase transformations in the oxide scale varying by temperature. Oxidation kinetics follow a parabolic rate law at 1200 -1500 ◦ C, governed by oxygen diffusion. Plasma ablation tests demonstrated exceptionally low ablation rates at 2100 ◦ C for 180 s (mass: 0.444 mg/s; linear: GLYPH<0> 0.079 μ m/ s), enhanced by self-healing via (Zr,Hf)SiO4 formation from SiO2 and (Zr,Hf,Ti)O2 reactions. Elemental mapping confirmed  preferential  oxidation  of  Hf/Zr  and  Ti  retention  via  SiO  co-evaporation.  These  synergistic  solidsolution and multiphase effects enhance oxidation and ablation resistance, demonstrating the material ' s suitability for durable UHTC thermal protection systems.

A B S T R A C T
Knowing the thermally and chemically aggressiveness of the environment  for  the  target  applications  of  MC-UHTC,  their  oxidation behavior  is  given  utmost  consideration.  Understanding  the  thermochemical  evolution  of  the  plasma  exposed  samples  is  necessary  to explain the surface properties and the phenomenon regulating the surface  oxidation  of  these  materials.  There  are  a  few  reports  on  the oxidation behavior of this new class of UHTCs [22 -28]. The oxidation of (Hf,Zr,Ta,Nb,Ti)C sample in the temperature range of 800 -1500 ◦ C (in the  air)  is  governed  by  a  diffusion-controlled  process  [22].  Another study [25] showed that the oxidation behavior of this composition was lower than that of unary ZrC by two orders of magnitude. Backman et al. [26,27] highlighted that the components ' preferential oxidation occurs in  MC-UHTCs using computational and experimental thermodynamic
Contents lists available at ScienceDirect

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have shown aspiration to overcome challenges in the thermal protection system (TPS) by designing new materials referred to as multi-component UHTCs (MC-UHTCs) in the compositional space. MC-UHTCs have shown remarkable improvement in oxidation resistance due to the formation of the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure during plasma exposure. Herein, the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure is synthesized via a solid-state reaction between HfO2 and Ta2O5 powder mixtures during spark plasma sintering (SPS). The compositions chosen are 50 vol% of HfO2 -50 vol% of Ta2O5 (50HO-50TO) and 70 vol% of HfO2 -30 vol% of Ta2O5 (70HO-30TO). The phase quantification via Rietveld analysis showed Hf6Ta2O17 as a principal phase with some residual Ta2O5  phase in both the samples. The high-temperature thermal stability of the samples was evaluated using high-velocity plasma jet exposure for up to 3 min. 50HO-50TO was able to withstand the intense plasma condition, which is attributed to the higher content of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase (~84%) and lower strain in the Ta2O5 phase. The augmentation in the Hf6Ta2O17 phase to 94.7% (in 50HO-50TO) post plasma exposure has been attributed to the invariant transformation from a liquid state to Hf6Ta2O17 at temperatures > 2500 ◦ C during testing. The mechanical integrity is elucidated from the insignificant change in the hardness ~13.3 GPa before and 11.2 GPa after plasma exposure of the 50HO-50TO sample. As a result, the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure ' s thermo-mechanical stability suggests developing novel oxidation-resistant MC-UHTCs in compositional space for reusable space vehicle applications.

Document: Nisar_2022_AppliedSurfaceScience_Quantificationofcomplex.pdf. Title: Quantification of complex protective surface oxide layer formed during plasma jet exposure of multicomponent ultra-high temperature carbides. The header and byline of this document reads: Full Length Article Ambreen Nisar a , Tamil Sakthivel b , Cheng Zhang a , Benjamin Boesl a , Sudipta Seal b , Arvind Agarwal a,* a Plasma Forming Laboratory, Department of Mechanical and Materials Engineering, Florida International University, Miami, FL 33174, USA Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Advanced Materials Processing Analysis Center, Nanoscience Technology Center, Biionix Cluster, College of Medicine, b University of Central Florida, Orlando, FL 32816, USA A R T I C L E  I N F O Keywords: Complex Surface Oxide Layer Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics (UHTCs) Multi-Component Thermal Protection System (TPS) Plasma Jet Exposure

A B S T R A C T
In  this  paper,  the  multi-scale  structures  of  Ta-Hf-W-C  ultra-high temperature  ceramic  coating  were  successfully  controlled  by  plasma process and post sintering treatment. (Ta, Hf, W)C single-phase, (Ta, Hf, C)/W nanocomposite, and (Ta, Hf, W)C/HfC micro-composite coatings were  achieved.  Coating  microstructures  and  forming  processes  were analyzed. DFT calculations elucidated the effects of cationic metallicity and carbon vacancy on crystal structure  and  coating  formation.  The mechanism of sintering-induced vertical cracking was explained using the phase field method. Coating structure-induced ablation performance variations and mechanisms were illustrated. Furthermore, a scale effect of oxidation, related to uniformity of metal elements in multicomponent ceramic coatings, was discovered. These findings offer critical insights for optimizing plasma sprayed UHTCs coating ablation performance.
